result: I keep thinking about the hour just before dawn, when the world is still technically night but the edges of everything have begun to remember they will soon be visible again. It’s the only time I feel the planet itself inhale. Streetlights don’t go off all at once; they nod out one by one like hosts after a party, unsure if the last guest has left. Somewhere a bakery light flicks on, yeast yawning into life. I am barefoot on the kitchen tiles, chasing the small, warm sound of the refrigerator, pretending I’m the only witness granted a backstage pass to the universe’s hush rehearsal. In that liminal glow I weigh the ethics of eating the last plum, its skin tight as a drum, knowing if I wait it will be too soft, if I bite I will be the singular agent of its perfection’s end. I bite. Juice performs a controlled fall down my wrist, proof that sweetness always has an exit strategy. Outside, a single sparrow practices the first note wrong, corrects, then nails it on the third try—reminding me that mastery is mostly willingness to begin before the sun validates the attempt. I think of every person I love as a muted constellation beyond the window, still sleeping, and I send them the spare silence I’m hoarding like emergency rations. It isn’t much, just 4 a.m.’s leftover hush folded into a paper boat, but it floats. When the sky finally cracks its thin gold seam, I feel the day click into its locked groove, and I carry the plum pit in my pocket like a dark little planet I’m not yet ready to bury.

I bite. Juice performs a controlled fall down my wrist, proof that sweetness always has an exit strategy. I think of every person I love as a muted constellation beyond the window, still sleeping, and I send them the spare silence I’m hoarding like emergency rations. When the sky finally cracks its thin gold seam, I feel the day click into its locked groove, and I carry the plum pit in my pocket like a dark little planet I’m not yet ready to bury.
